(locally [/ˈkwiːnzlænd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KWEENZ-land*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-9) commonly abbreviated as **QLD**) is a [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") in northeastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the second-largest and third-most populous state in Australia. It is bordered by the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") to the west, south-west and south, respectively. To the east, Queensland is bordered by the [Coral Sea](/wiki/Coral_Sea "Coral Sea") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"); to the state's north is the [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait"), separating the Australian mainland from [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), and the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria") to the north-west. With an area of 1,723,030 square kilometres (665,270 sq mi), Queensland is the world's [sixth-largest subdivision](/wiki/List_of_country_subdivisions_by_area "List of country subdivisions by area") of any country on earth; it [is larger than all but 16 countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"). Due to its size, Queensland's geographical features and climates are diverse, and include [tropical rainforests](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"), [rivers](/wiki/River "River"), [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef"), [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") and white sandy beaches in its [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") and [sub-tropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") coastal regions, as well as [deserts](/wiki/Desert "Desert") and [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") in the [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") and [desert](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate") climatic regions of its [interior](/wiki/Outback "Outback").

**Queensland** (locally [/ˈkwiːnzlænd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KWEENZ-land*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-9) commonly abbreviated as **QLD**) is a [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") in northeastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the second-largest and third-most populous state in Australia. It is bordered by the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") to the west, south-west and south, respectively. To the east, Queensland is bordered by the [Coral Sea](/wiki/Coral_Sea "Coral Sea") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"); to the state's north is the [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait"), separating the Australian mainland from [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea"), and the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria") to the north-west. With an area of 1,723,030 square kilometres (665,270 sq mi), Queensland is the world's [sixth-largest subdivision](/wiki/List_of_country_subdivisions_by_area "List of country subdivisions by area") of any country on earth; it [is larger than all but 16 countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"). Due to its size, Queensland's geographical features and climates are diverse, and include [tropical rainforests](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"), [rivers](/wiki/River "River"), [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef"), [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") and white sandy beaches in its [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") and [sub-tropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") coastal regions, as well as [deserts](/wiki/Desert "Desert") and [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") in the [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") and [desert](/wiki/Desert_climate "Desert climate") climatic regions of its [interior](/wiki/Outback "Outback").

Queensland has a population of over 5.5 million,[[9]](#cite_note-ABSPop-10) concentrated in [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland"), where nearly three in four reside. The capital and largest city in the state is [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), Australia's [third-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population") and comprising fully half of the state's population. Ten of Australia's thirty largest cities are located in Queensland, the largest outside Brisbane being the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland"), the [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland"), [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville"), [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns"), [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich,_Queensland "Ipswich, Queensland"), and [Toowoomba](/wiki/Toowoomba "Toowoomba"). 24.2% of the state's population were [born overseas](/wiki/Immigration_to_Australia "Immigration to Australia").[[10]](#cite_note-11) The state has the highest inter-state net migration in Australia.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Queensland was first inhabited by [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians"), with the [Torres Strait Islands](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islands "Torres Strait Islands") inhabited by [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders").[[12]](#cite_note-13) Dutch navigator [Willem Janszoon](/wiki/Willem_Janszoon "Willem Janszoon"), the first European to land in Australia, explored the west coast of the [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula") in 1606. In 1770, [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") claimed the east coast of Australia for the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). In 1788, [Arthur Phillip](/wiki/Arthur_Phillip "Arthur Phillip") founded the colony of New South Wales, which included all of what is now Queensland. Queensland was explored in subsequent decades, and the [Moreton Bay Penal Settlement](/wiki/Moreton_Bay_Penal_Settlement "Moreton Bay Penal Settlement") was established at Brisbane in 1824 by [John Oxley](/wiki/John_Oxley "John Oxley"). During the [Australian frontier wars](/wiki/Australian_frontier_wars "Australian frontier wars") of the 19th century, colonists killed tens of thousands of Aboriginal people in Queensland while consolidating their control over the territory.

On 6 June 1859 (now commemorated as [Queensland Day](/wiki/Queensland_Day "Queensland Day")), [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") signed the letters patent to establish the colony of Queensland, [separating it from New South Wales](/wiki/Separation_of_Queensland "Separation of Queensland") and thereby establishing Queensland as a [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") [Crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") with [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"). A large part of colonial Queensland's economy relied on [blackbirded](/wiki/Blackbirding "Blackbirding") South Sea Islander slavery.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Queensland was among the six colonies which became the founding states of Australia with [Federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") on 1 January 1901. Since the [Bjelke-Petersen](/wiki/Joh_Bjelke-Petersen "Joh Bjelke-Petersen") era of the late 20th century, Queensland has received a high level of internal migration from the other states and territories of Australia and remains a popular destination for interstate migration.

Queensland has the [third-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_Australian_states_and_territories_by_gross_state_product "List of Australian states and territories by gross state product") among Australian states, with strengths in mining, agriculture, transportation, [international education](/wiki/International_student "International student"), insurance, and banking. Nicknamed the *Sunshine State* for its tropical and sub-tropical climates, [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), and numerous beaches, tourism is also important to the state's economy.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Queensland](/wiki/History_of_Queensland "History of Queensland")

### Pre-European contact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-European contact")]

Main article: [History of Indigenous Australians](/wiki/History_of_Indigenous_Australians "History of Indigenous Australians")

Queensland was one of the largest regions of pre-colonial Aboriginal population in Australia.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEØrsted-Jensen201110–11-14) The Aboriginal ownership of Queensland is thought to predate 50,000 BC, and early migrants are believed to have arrived via boat or land bridge across [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait"). Through time, their descendants developed into more than 90 different language and cultural groups.

During the last [ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age"), Queensland's landscape became more arid and largely desolate, making food and other supplies scarce. The people developed the world's first seed-grinding technology.[[14]](#cite_note-15) The end of the [glacial period](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period") brought about a warming climate, making the land more hospitable. It brought high rainfall along the eastern coast, stimulating the growth of the state's tropical rainforests.[[15]](#cite_note-r-evans-hoq-16)

The [Torres Strait Islands](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islands "Torres Strait Islands") is home to the [Torres Strait Islander peoples](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"). Torres Strait Islanders are ethnically and culturally distinct from mainland Aboriginal peoples. They have a long history of interaction with both Aboriginal peoples of what is now Australia and the peoples of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea").

### European colonisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: European colonisation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Captain_Cook_at_Possession_Island.jpg/330px-Captain_Cook_at_Possession_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Captain_Cook_at_Possession_Island.jpg)

Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") claims the [east coast of Australia](/wiki/Eastern_states_of_Australia "Eastern states of Australia") for the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") at [Possession Island](/wiki/Possession_Island_(Queensland) "Possession Island (Queensland)") in 1770

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/SeventeenSeventyNov082024_01.jpg/330px-SeventeenSeventyNov082024_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:SeventeenSeventyNov082024_01.jpg)

The peninsula of [Seventeen Seventy, Queensland](/wiki/Seventeen_Seventy,_Queensland "Seventeen Seventy, Queensland"), where Captain Cook landed in 1770

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Bulla_Queensland_1861.jpg/330px-Bulla_Queensland_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bulla_Queensland_1861.jpg)

Fighting between [Burke and Wills](/wiki/Burke_and_Wills_expedition "Burke and Wills expedition")'s supply party and Aboriginal Australians at Bulla in 1861

In February 1606, Dutch navigator [Willem Janszoon](/wiki/Willem_Janszoon "Willem Janszoon") landed near the site of what is now [Weipa](/wiki/Weipa,_Queensland "Weipa, Queensland"), on the western shore of [Cape York](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula"). This was the first recorded landing of a European in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and it also marked the first reported contact between Europeans and the [Aboriginal people of Australia](/wiki/Aboriginal_people_of_Australia "Aboriginal people of Australia").[[15]](#cite_note-r-evans-hoq-16) The region was also explored by French and Spanish explorers (commanded by [Louis Antoine de Bougainville](/wiki/Louis_Antoine_de_Bougainville "Louis Antoine de Bougainville") and [Luís Vaez de Torres](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Vaez_de_Torres "Luís Vaez de Torres"), respectively) before the arrival of Lieutenant [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") in 1770. Cook claimed the east coast under instruction from [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") of the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") on 22 August 1770 at [Possession Island](/wiki/Possession_Island_(Queensland) "Possession Island (Queensland)"), naming eastern Australia, including Queensland, *New South Wales*.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The Aboriginal population declined significantly after a [smallpox epidemic](/wiki/History_of_smallpox#Pacific_epidemics "History of smallpox") during the late 18th century and massacres by the European settlers.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In 1823, [John Oxley](/wiki/John_Oxley "John Oxley"), a British explorer, sailed north from what is now [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") to scout possible penal colony sites in [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone,_Queensland "Gladstone, Queensland") (then [Port Curtis](/wiki/Port_Curtis "Port Curtis")) and [Moreton Bay](/wiki/Moreton_Bay "Moreton Bay"). At Moreton Bay, he found the [Brisbane River](/wiki/Brisbane_River "Brisbane River"). He returned in 1824 and established a penal settlement at what is now [Redcliffe](/wiki/Redcliffe_Peninsula "Redcliffe Peninsula"). The settlement, initially known as [Edenglassie](/wiki/Edenglassie "Edenglassie"), was then transferred to the current location of the [Brisbane city centre](/wiki/Brisbane_central_business_district "Brisbane central business district"). [Edmund Lockyer](/wiki/Edmund_Lockyer "Edmund Lockyer") discovered outcrops of coal along the banks of the upper Brisbane River in 1825.[[18]](#cite_note-19) In 1839 transportation of convicts was ceased, culminating in the closure of the Brisbane penal settlement. In 1842 free settlement, which had already commenced, was officially permitted. In 1847, the [Port of Maryborough](/wiki/Port_of_Maryborough "Port of Maryborough") was opened as a wool port. While most early immigrants came from New South Wales, the first free immigrant ship to arrive in Moreton Bay from Europe was the [*Artemisia*](/wiki/Artemisia_(ship) "Artemisia (ship)"), in 1848.

Earlier than this immigrant ship was the arrival of the Irish famine orphan girls to Queensland. Devised by the then British Secretary of State for the Colonies, The Earl Grey Scheme established a special emigration scheme which was designed to resettle destitute girls from the workhouses of Ireland during the Great Famine. The first ship, the "Earl Grey", departed Ireland for a 124-day sail to Sydney. After controversy developed upon their arrival in Australia, a small group of 37 young orphans, sometimes referred to as The Belfast Girls or the Feisty Colleens, never set foot on Sydney soil, and instead sailed up to Brisbane (then Moreton Bay) on 21 October 1848 on board the *Ann Mary*. This scheme continued until 1852.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

In 1857, Queensland's first lighthouse was built at [Cape Moreton](/wiki/Cape_Moreton "Cape Moreton").[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Frontier wars and massacres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Frontier wars and massacres")]

Further information: [Australian frontier wars § Queensland](/wiki/Australian_frontier_wars#Queensland "Australian frontier wars"), and [List of massacres of Indigenous Australians § Queensland](/wiki/List_of_massacres_of_Indigenous_Australians#Queensland "List of massacres of Indigenous Australians")

The frontier wars fought between European settlers and Aboriginal tribes in Queensland were the bloodiest and most brutal in colonial Australia.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Many of these conflicts are now seen as acts of genocide.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-queteen-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The wars featured the most frequent massacres of First Nations people, the three deadliest massacres on white settlers, the most disreputable frontier police force, and the highest number of white victims to frontier violence on record in any Australian colony.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEØrsted-Jensen2011-27) Across at least 644 collisions at least 66,680 were killed — with Aboriginal fatalities alone comprising no less than 65,180.[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-28) Of these deaths, around 24,000 Aboriginal men, women and children were killed by the Native Police between 1859 and 1897.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

The military force of the Queensland Government in this war was the [Native Police](/wiki/Australian_native_police "Australian native police"), who operated from 1849 to the 1920s. The Native Police was a body of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander troopers that operated under the command of white officers. The Native Police were often recruited forcefully from far-away communities.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Wills_Tragedy_1861.jpg/250px-Wills_Tragedy_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wills_Tragedy_1861.jpg)

Aftermath of the 1861 [Cullin-La-Ringo massacre](/wiki/Cullin-La-Ringo_massacre "Cullin-La-Ringo massacre") in which 19 settlers were killed by Aboriginal people, the deadliest attack on settlers in the frontier wars

Conflict spread quickly with free settlement in 1838, with settlement rapidly expanding in a great rush to take up the surrounding land in the [Darling Downs](/wiki/Darling_Downs "Darling Downs"), Logan and Brisbane Valley and South Burnett onwards from 1840, in many cases leading to widespread fighting and heavy loss of life. The conflict later spread north to the [Wide Bay](/wiki/Wide_Bay%E2%80%93Burnett "Wide Bay–Burnett") and [Burnett River](/wiki/Burnett_River "Burnett River") and [Hervey Bay](/wiki/Hervey_Bay "Hervey Bay") region, and at one stage the settlement of [Maryborough](/wiki/Maryborough,_Queensland "Maryborough, Queensland") was virtually under siege.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBroome1988102-32)

The largest reasonably well-documented massacres in southeast Queensland were the [Kilcoy](/wiki/Kilcoy,_Queensland "Kilcoy, Queensland") and [Whiteside](/wiki/Whiteside,_Queensland "Whiteside, Queensland") poisonings, each of which was said to have taken up to 70 Aboriginal lives by use of a gift of flour laced with [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine"). At the [Battle of One Tree Hill](/wiki/Battle_of_One_Tree_Hill "Battle of One Tree Hill") in September 1843, [Multuggerah](/wiki/Multuggerah "Multuggerah") and his group of warriors ambushed one group of settlers, routing them and subsequently others in the skirmishes which followed, starting in retaliation for the Kilcoy poisoning.[[32]](#cite_note-kerkhove-33)[[33]](#cite_note-marr-34)

Central Queensland was particularly hard hit during the 1860s and 1870s, several contemporary writers mention the Skull Hole, Bladensburg, or Mistake Creek massacre[[a]](#cite_note-35) on [Bladensburg Station](/wiki/Bladensburg_Station "Bladensburg Station") near [Winton](/wiki/Winton,_Queensland "Winton, Queensland"), which in 1901 was said to have taken up to 200 Aboriginal lives.[[34]](#cite_note-36) First Nations warriors killed 19 settlers during the [Cullin-La-Ringo massacre](/wiki/Cullin-La-Ringo_massacre "Cullin-La-Ringo massacre") on 17 October 1861.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnor2008220-37) In the weeks afterwards, police, native police and civilians killed up to 370 members of the [Gayiri](/wiki/Gayiri "Gayiri") Aboriginal people in response.[[36]](#cite_note-Jackson-38)

Frontier violence peaked on the northern mining frontier during the 1870s, most notably in Cook district and on the [Palmer](/wiki/Palmer_River_(Queensland) "Palmer River (Queensland)") and Hodgkinson River goldfields, with heavy loss of Aboriginal lives and several well-known massacres.[[37]](#cite_note-39) Raids conducted by the [Kalkadoon](/wiki/Kalkatungu "Kalkatungu") held settlers out of Western Queensland for ten years until September 1884 when they attacked a force of settlers and native police at Battle Mountain near modern [Cloncurry](/wiki/Cloncurry,_Queensland "Cloncurry, Queensland"). The subsequent battle of Battle Mountain ended in disaster for the Kalkadoon, who suffered heavy losses.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoulthard-Clark200151–52-40) Fighting continued in [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland"), however, with First Nations raiders attacking sheep and cattle while Native Police mounted heavy retaliatory massacres.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnor2008221-41)[[40]](#cite_note-42)

### Blackbirding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Blackbirding")]

Further information: [Blackbirding § Queensland](/wiki/Blackbirding#Queensland "Blackbirding")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Groupe_de_Kanakas_dans_une_exploitation_de_canne_%C3%A0_sucre_du_Queensland.jpg/250px-Groupe_de_Kanakas_dans_une_exploitation_de_canne_%C3%A0_sucre_du_Queensland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Groupe_de_Kanakas_dans_une_exploitation_de_canne_%C3%A0_sucre_du_Queensland.jpg)

[Kanaka workers](/wiki/Kanaka_(Pacific_Island_worker) "Kanaka (Pacific Island worker)") in a sugar cane plantation, late 19th century

Tens of thousands of [South Sea Islanders](/wiki/South_Sea_Islanders "South Sea Islanders") were forced, deceived or coerced into [indentured servitude](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude") and slavery on Australia's agricultural plantations, despite slavery being outlawed in Australia and other parts of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") by the [Slavery Abolition Act 1833](/wiki/Slavery_Abolition_Act_1833 "Slavery Abolition Act 1833"). This process was known as blackbirding.[[41]](#cite_note-Mortensen_2000-43)[[42]](#cite_note-McDonald_2023-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45) This trade in what were then known as [Kanakas](/wiki/Kanaka_(Pacific_Island_worker) "Kanaka (Pacific Island worker)") was in operation from 1863 to 1908, a period of 45 years. Some 55,000 to 62,500 were brought to Australia, most being recruited or blackbirded from islands in [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia"), such as the [New Hebrides](/wiki/New_Hebrides "New Hebrides") (now [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu")), the [Solomon Islands](/wiki/Solomon_Islands "Solomon Islands") and the islands around [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea").[[44]](#cite_note-AHRC-46)

[![South Sea Islander men standing in front of a row of sugarcane.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Australian_South_Sea_Islander_cane_cutters_on_a_sugar_cane_plantation_in_Queensland_%289205204184%29.jpg/250px-Australian_South_Sea_Islander_cane_cutters_on_a_sugar_cane_plantation_in_Queensland_%289205204184%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australian_South_Sea_Islander_cane_cutters_on_a_sugar_cane_plantation_in_Queensland_(9205204184).jpg)

Blackbirded South Sea Islanders on a [Sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane") plantation in Queensland

The majority of those taken were male and around one quarter were under the age of sixteen.[[45]](#cite_note-47) In total, approximately 15,000 South Sea Islanders (30%) died while labouring in Queensland – excluding those who died in transit or were killed in the recruitment process – mostly during three-year contracts.[[46]](#cite_note-48) This is similar to the estimated 33% death rate among enslaved Africans in the first three years of arriving in America,[[47]](#cite_note-49) Brazil, and the Caribbean; the conditions were often comparable to those of the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade").[[42]](#cite_note-McDonald_2023-44)[[41]](#cite_note-Mortensen_2000-43)

The trade was legally sanctioned and regulated under Queensland law, and prominent men such as Robert Towns made massive fortunes through blackbirding, helping to establish some of the major cities in Queensland today.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Towns' agent claimed that blackbirded labourers were "savages who did not know the use of money" and therefore did not deserve cash wages.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Following Federation in 1901, the [White Australia policy](/wiki/White_Australia_policy "White Australia policy") came into effect, which saw most foreign workers in Australia deported under the *[Pacific Island Labourers Act 1901](/wiki/Pacific_Island_Labourers_Act_1901 "Pacific Island Labourers Act 1901")*, which saw the Pacific Islander population of the state decrease rapidly.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

### Independent governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Independent governance")]

Further information: [Colony of Queensland](/wiki/Colony_of_Queensland "Colony of Queensland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/StateLibQld_1_14202_Shearers_and_farm_hands_at_Jondaryan_Woolshed%2C_ca._1872.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_14202_Shearers_and_farm_hands_at_Jondaryan_Woolshed%2C_ca._1872.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_14202_Shearers_and_farm_hands_at_Jondaryan_Woolshed,_ca._1872.jpg)

[Sheep shearers](/wiki/Sheep_shearing "Sheep shearing") and farm hands at [Jondaryan Woolshed](/wiki/Jondaryan_Woolshed "Jondaryan Woolshed"), c. 1872

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/StateLibQld_2_93580_Parade_of_troops_in_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_March%2C_1900.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_2_93580_Parade_of_troops_in_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_March%2C_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_2_93580_Parade_of_troops_in_Queen_Street,_Brisbane,_March,_1900.jpg)

Parade of troops in Brisbane, prior to departure for the [Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") in South Africa

A public meeting was held in 1851 to consider the proposed [separation of Queensland](/wiki/Separation_of_Queensland "Separation of Queensland") from New South Wales. On 6 June 1859, Queen Victoria signed letters patent[[51]](#cite_note-53) to form the separate colony of Queensland as a [self-governing](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") [Crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") with [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government"). [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") was selected as the capital city. On 10 December 1859, a proclamation was read by [George Bowen](/wiki/George_Bowen "George Bowen"), the first [Governor of Queensland](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland"), formally establishing Queensland as a separate colony from New South Wales.[[52]](#cite_note-q150-54) On 22 May 1860 the first Queensland election was held and [Robert Herbert](/wiki/Robert_Herbert "Robert Herbert"), Bowen's private secretary, was appointed as the first [Premier of Queensland](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland").

In 1865, the first rail line in the state opened between [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich,_Queensland "Ipswich, Queensland") and [Grandchester](/wiki/Grandchester "Grandchester"). Queensland's economy expanded rapidly in 1867 after James Nash discovered gold on the [Mary River](/wiki/Mary_River_(Queensland) "Mary River (Queensland)") near the town of [Gympie](/wiki/Gympie "Gympie"), sparking a gold rush and saving the Colony of Queensland from near economic collapse. While still significant, they were on a much smaller scale than the gold rushes of Victoria and New South Wales.

Immigration to Australia and Queensland, in particular, began in the 1850s to support the state economy. During the period from the 1860s until the early 20th century, many labourers, known at the time as [Kanakas](/wiki/Kanakas "Kanakas"), were brought to Queensland from neighbouring Pacific Island nations to work in the state's sugar cane fields. Some of these people had been kidnapped under a process known as [blackbirding](/wiki/Blackbirding "Blackbirding") or press-ganging, and their employment conditions constituted an allegedly exploitative form of indentured labour. [Italian immigrants](/wiki/Italian_Australians "Italian Australians") entered the sugar cane industry from the 1890s.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

During the 1890s, the six Australian colonies, including Queensland, held a series of referendums which culminated in the [Federation of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") on 1 January 1901. During this time, Queensland had a population of half a million people. Since then, Queensland has remained a [federated state](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state") within Australia, and its population has significantly grown.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/StateLibQld_1_114168_Returned_World_War_Two_soldiers_march_in_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_1944.jpg/250px-StateLibQld_1_114168_Returned_World_War_Two_soldiers_march_in_Queen_Street%2C_Brisbane%2C_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:StateLibQld_1_114168_Returned_World_War_Two_soldiers_march_in_Queen_Street,_Brisbane,_1944.jpg)

Returned World War II soldiers march in Queen Street, Brisbane, 1944

In 1905 women voted in state elections for the first time. The state's first university, the [University of Queensland](/wiki/University_of_Queensland "University of Queensland"), was established in Brisbane in 1909. In 1911, the first alternative treatments for polio were pioneered in Queensland and remain in use across the world today.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") had a [major impact on Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_in_World_War_I "Queensland in World War I"). Over 58,000 Queenslanders fought in World War I and over 10,000 of them died.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

Australia's first major airline, [Qantas](/wiki/Qantas "Qantas") (originally standing for "Queensland and Northern Territory Aerial Services"), was founded in [Winton](/wiki/Winton,_Queensland "Winton, Queensland") in 1920 to serve outback Queensland.

In 1922 Queensland abolished the [Queensland Legislative Council](/wiki/Queensland_Legislative_Council "Queensland Legislative Council"), becoming the only Australian state with a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral_legislature "Unicameral legislature") [parliament](/wiki/Parliaments_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Parliaments of the Australian states and territories").

In 1935 [cane toads](/wiki/Cane_toads_in_Australia "Cane toads in Australia") were deliberately introduced to Queensland from Hawaii in an unsuccessful attempt to reduce the number of French's cane and greyback [cane beetles](/wiki/Cane_beetle "Cane beetle") that were destroying the roots of sugar cane plants, which are integral to Queensland's economy. The toads have remained an environmental pest since that time. In 1962, the first commercial production of oil in Queensland and Australia began at [Moonie](/wiki/Moonie,_Queensland "Moonie, Queensland").

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") Brisbane became central to the [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") campaign when the AMP Building (now called [MacArthur Central](/wiki/MacArthur_Central "MacArthur Central")) was used as the [South West Pacific headquarters](/wiki/South_West_Pacific_Area_(command)#Command "South West Pacific Area (command)") for [General Douglas MacArthur](/wiki/Douglas_MacArthur "Douglas MacArthur"), chief of the Allied Pacific forces, until his headquarters were moved to [Hollandia](/wiki/Jayapura "Jayapura") in August 1944.[[56]](#cite_note-58) In 1942, during the war, Brisbane was the site of a violent clash between visiting US military personnel and Australian servicemen and civilians, which resulted in one death and hundreds of injuries. This incident became known colloquially as the [Battle of Brisbane](/wiki/Battle_of_Brisbane "Battle of Brisbane").[[57]](#cite_note-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/ITM436209_Dept_No_404_%2851763920364%29.jpg/250px-ITM436209_Dept_No_404_%2851763920364%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ITM436209_Dept_No_404_(51763920364).jpg)

[Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") visited Brisbane in October 1982 for the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games")

The end of World War II saw a [wave of immigration](/wiki/Post-war_immigration_to_Australia "Post-war immigration to Australia") from across Europe, with many more immigrants coming from [southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and [eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") Europe than in previous decades.

In the later decades of the 20th century, the [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate")—regulated by the availability of air conditioning—saw Queensland become a popular destination for migrants from interstate.[[58]](#cite_note-60) Since that time, Queensland has continuously seen high levels of migration from the other states and territories of Australia.

In 1966, [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") became the first U.S. president to visit Queensland. During his visit, he met with Australia prime minister [Harold Holt](/wiki/Harold_Holt "Harold Holt").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The end of the [White Australia policy](/wiki/White_Australia_policy "White Australia policy") in 1973 saw the beginning of a wave of immigration from around the world, and most prominently from Asia, which continues to the present.

In 1981 the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef") off Queensland's northeast coast, one of the world's largest coral reef systems, was declared a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2003 Queensland adopted [maroon](/wiki/Maroon "Maroon") as the state's official colour. The announcement was made as a result of an informal tradition to use maroon to represent the state in association with sporting events.[[6]](#cite_note-state-colour-6)

After three decades of record population growth, Queensland was impacted by major [floods between late 2010 and early 2011](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Queensland_floods "2010–2011 Queensland floods"), causing extensive damage and disruption across the state.[[59]](#cite_note-Berry-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)

In 2020, Queensland was impacted by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). Despite a [low number and abrupt decline in cases](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Australia "COVID-19 pandemic in Australia") from April 2020 onward, [social distancing](/wiki/Social_distancing "Social distancing") requirements were implemented from March 2020 including the closure of the state borders.

## Geography

With a total area of 1,729,742 square kilometres (715,309 square miles), Queensland is an expansive state with a highly diverse range of climates and geographical features. If Queensland were an independent nation, it would be the world's 16th largest in terms of area.

Queensland's eastern coastline borders the [Coral Sea](/wiki/Coral_Sea "Coral Sea"), an arm of the Pacific Ocean. The state is bordered by the [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait") to the north, with [Boigu Island](/wiki/Boigu_Island_(Queensland) "Boigu Island (Queensland)") off the coast of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") representing the northern extreme of its territory. The triangular [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula"), which points toward New Guinea, is the northernmost part of the state's mainland. West of the peninsula's tip, northern Queensland is bordered by the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria"). To the west, Queensland is bordered by the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"), at the [138th meridian east](/wiki/138th_meridian_east "138th meridian east"), and to the southwest by northeastern [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"). The state's southern border with New South Wales is constituted in the east by the [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") from [Point Danger](/wiki/Point_Danger_(Tweed_Heads) "Point Danger (Tweed Heads)") to the [Dumaresq River](/wiki/Dumaresq_River "Dumaresq River"), and the Dumaresq, [Macintyre](/wiki/Macintyre_River "Macintyre River") and [Barwon](/wiki/Barwon_River_(New_South_Wales) "Barwon River (New South Wales)") rivers. The west of the southern border is defined by the [29th parallel south](/wiki/29th_parallel_south "29th parallel south") (including some minor [historical encroachments](/wiki/Queensland_and_New_South_Wales_boundary_encroachments "Queensland and New South Wales boundary encroachments")) until it reaches South Australia.

Like much of eastern Australia, the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range") runs roughly parallel with, and inland from, the coast, and areas west of the range are more arid than the humid coastal regions. The [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), which is the world's largest [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") system, runs parallel to the state's [Coral Sea](/wiki/Coral_Sea "Coral Sea") coast between the [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait") and [K'gari (Fraser Island)](/wiki/K%27gari_(Fraser_Island) "K'gari (Fraser Island)"). Queensland's coastline includes the world's three largest sand islands: [K'gari (Fraser Island)](/wiki/K%27gari_(Fraser_Island) "K'gari (Fraser Island)"), [Moreton](/wiki/Moreton_Island "Moreton Island"), and [North Stradbroke](/wiki/North_Stradbroke_Island "North Stradbroke Island").

The state contains six [World Heritage](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")-listed preservation areas: the Great Barrier Reef along the Coral Sea coast, K'gari (Fraser Island) on the [Wide Bay–Burnett](/wiki/Wide_Bay%E2%80%93Burnett "Wide Bay–Burnett") region's coastline, the [wet tropics](/wiki/Wet_Tropics_of_Queensland "Wet Tropics of Queensland") in [Far North Queensland](/wiki/Far_North_Queensland "Far North Queensland") including the [Daintree Rainforest](/wiki/Daintree_Rainforest "Daintree Rainforest"), [Lamington National Park](/wiki/Lamington_National_Park "Lamington National Park") in [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland"), the [Riversleigh fossil sites](/wiki/Australian_Fossil_Mammal_Sites_(Riversleigh) "Australian Fossil Mammal Sites (Riversleigh)") in [North West Queensland](/wiki/Gulf_Country "Gulf Country"), and the [Gondwana Rainforests](/wiki/Gondwana_Rainforests "Gondwana Rainforests") in South East Queensland.

The state is divided into several [unofficial regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Queensland "Regions of Queensland") which are commonly used to refer to large areas of the state's vast geography. These include:

* **[South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland")** in the state's coastal extreme south-eastern corner, an urban region which includes the state's three largest cities: capital city Brisbane and popular coastal tourist destinations the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") and [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland"). In some definitions, it also includes the city of [Toowoomba](/wiki/Toowoomba,_Queensland "Toowoomba, Queensland"). South East Queensland accounts for more than 70% of the state's population.
* The **[Darling Downs](/wiki/Darling_Downs "Darling Downs")** in the state's inland southeast, which consists of fertile agricultural (particularly cattle grazing) land and in some definitions includes the city of Toowoomba. The region also includes the mountainous [Granite Belt](/wiki/Granite_Belt "Granite Belt"), the state's coldest region which occasionally experiences snow.
* **[Wide Bay–Burnett](/wiki/Wide_Bay%E2%80%93Burnett "Wide Bay–Burnett")** in the state's coastal southeast, to the north of the South East Queensland region. It is rich in [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane") farms and includes the cities of [Bundaberg](/wiki/Bundaberg "Bundaberg"), [Hervey Bay](/wiki/Hervey_Bay "Hervey Bay") as well as [K'gari (Fraser Island)](/wiki/K%27gari_(Fraser_Island) "K'gari (Fraser Island)"), the world's largest sand island.
* **[Central Queensland](/wiki/Central_Queensland "Central Queensland")** on the state's central coastline, which is dominated by cattle farmland and coal mining. It contains the [Capricorn Coast](/wiki/Capricorn_Coast "Capricorn Coast") and [Whitsunday Islands](/wiki/Whitsunday_Islands "Whitsunday Islands") tourist regions, as well as the cities of [Rockhampton](/wiki/Rockhampton "Rockhampton") and [Mackay](/wiki/Mackay,_Queensland "Mackay, Queensland").
* **[North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland")** on the state's northern coastline, which is dominated by cattle farmland and mining and which includes the city of [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville").
* **[Far North Queensland](/wiki/Far_North_Queensland "Far North Queensland")** on the state's extreme northern coastline along the [Cape York Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_York_Peninsula "Cape York Peninsula"), which includes [tropical rainforest](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest "Tropical rainforest"), the state's highest mountain, [Mount Bartle Frere](/wiki/Mount_Bartle_Frere "Mount Bartle Frere"), the [Atherton Tablelands](/wiki/Atherton_Tablelands "Atherton Tablelands") pastoral region (dominated by [sugar cane](/wiki/Sugar_cane "Sugar cane") and [tropical fruits](/wiki/Tropical_fruit "Tropical fruit")), the most visited section of the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), as well as the city of [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns,_Queensland "Cairns, Queensland").
* **[South West Queensland](/wiki/South_West_Queensland "South West Queensland")** in the state's inland south-west, which is a primarily agricultural region dominated by cattle farmland, and which includes the [Channel Country](/wiki/Channel_Country "Channel Country") region of [intertwining rivulets](/wiki/Braided_river "Braided river").
* **[Central West Queensland](/wiki/Central_West_Queensland "Central West Queensland")** in the state's inland central-west, dominated by cattle farmland and which includes the city of [Longreach](/wiki/Longreach,_Queensland "Longreach, Queensland").
* The **[Gulf Country](/wiki/Gulf_Country "Gulf Country")** (also known as North West Queensland), in the state's inland north-west along the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria"), which is dominated by [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") and mining and includes the [city of Mount Isa](/wiki/City_of_Mount_Isa "City of Mount Isa").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Queensland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Queensland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Queensland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in Queensland

Because of its size, there is significant variation in climate across the state. There is ample rainfall along the coastline, with a [monsoonal](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") wet season in the [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") north, and [humid sub-tropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") conditions along the southern coastline. Low rainfall and hot humid summers are typical for the inland and west. Elevated areas in the south-eastern inland can experience temperatures well below freezing in mid-winter providing [frost](/wiki/Frost "Frost") and, rarely, [snowfall](/wiki/Snowfall "Snowfall"). The climate of the coastal regions is influenced by warm ocean waters, keeping the region free from extremes of temperature and providing moisture for rainfall.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

There are six predominant climatic zones in Queensland,[[62]](#cite_note-64) based on temperature and humidity:

* Hot humid summer, warm humid winter (far north and coastal): [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns"), [Innisfail](/wiki/Innisfail,_Queensland "Innisfail, Queensland")
* Hot humid summer, warm dry winter (north and coastal): [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville"), [Mackay](/wiki/Mackay,_Queensland "Mackay, Queensland")
* Hot humid summer, mild dry winter (coastal elevated areas and coastal south-east): [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Bundaberg](/wiki/Bundaberg "Bundaberg"), [Rockhampton](/wiki/Rockhampton "Rockhampton")
* Hot dry summer, mild dry winter (central inland and north-west): [Mt Isa](/wiki/Mt_Isa "Mt Isa"), [Emerald](/wiki/Emerald,_Queensland "Emerald, Queensland"), [Longreach](/wiki/Longreach "Longreach")
* Hot dry summer, cool dry winter (southern inland): [Roma](/wiki/Roma,_Queensland "Roma, Queensland"), [Charleville](/wiki/Charleville,_Queensland "Charleville, Queensland"), [Goondiwindi](/wiki/Goondiwindi "Goondiwindi")
* Warm humid summer, cold dry winter (elevated south-eastern areas): [Toowoomba](/wiki/Toowoomba "Toowoomba"), [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_Queensland "Warwick, Queensland"), [Stanthorpe](/wiki/Stanthorpe "Stanthorpe")

The coastal far north of the state is the wettest region in Australia, with [Mount Bellenden Ker](/wiki/Mount_Bellenden_Ker "Mount Bellenden Ker"), south of Cairns, holding many Australian rainfall records with its annual average rainfall of over 8 metres (26 ft).[[68]](#cite_note-70) Snow is rare in Queensland, although it does fall with some regularity along the far southern border with New South Wales, predominantly in the Stanthorpe district although on rare occasions further north and west. The most northerly snow ever recorded in Australia occurred near [Mackay](/wiki/Mackay,_Queensland "Mackay, Queensland"); however, this was exceptional.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

Natural disasters are often a threat in Queensland: severe [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") can impact the central and northern coastlines and cause severe damage,[[70]](#cite_note-72) with recent examples including [Larry](/wiki/Cyclone_Larry "Cyclone Larry"), [Yasi](/wiki/Cyclone_Yasi "Cyclone Yasi"), [Ita](/wiki/Cyclone_Ita "Cyclone Ita") and [Debbie](/wiki/Cyclone_Debbie "Cyclone Debbie"). Flooding from rain-bearing systems can also be severe and can occur anywhere in Queensland. One of the deadliest and most damaging floods in the history of the state occurred in [early 2011](/wiki/2010-11_Queensland_floods "2010-11 Queensland floods").[[71]](#cite_note-73) Severe springtime [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") generally affect the south-east and inland of the state and can bring damaging winds, torrential rain, large [hail](/wiki/Hail "Hail") and even [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado").[[72]](#cite_note-74) The [strongest tornado](/wiki/Bucca_tornado "Bucca tornado") ever recorded in Australia occurred in Queensland near [Bundaberg](/wiki/Bundaberg "Bundaberg") in November 1992.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Droughts and [bushfires](/wiki/Bushfires_in_Australia "Bushfires in Australia") can also occur; however, the latter are generally less severe than those that occur in southern states.

The highest official maximum temperature recorded in the state was 49.5 °C (121.1 °F) at Birdsville Police Station on 24 December 1972.[[74]](#cite_note-76) The lowest recorded minimum temperature is −10.6 °C (12.9 °F) at Stanthorpe on 23 June 1961 and at The Hermitage (near [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick,_Queensland "Warwick, Queensland")) on 12 July 1965.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

In December 2021, Queensland had an estimated population of 5,265,043.[[9]](#cite_note-ABSPop-10) Approximately half of the state's population lives in Brisbane, and over 70% live in [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland"). Nonetheless, Queensland is the second most decentralised state in Australia after [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"). Since the 1980s, Queensland has consistently been the fastest-growing state in Australia, as it receives high levels of both international immigration and migration from interstate. There have however been short periods where [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") and [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") have grown faster.

Early settlers during the 19th century were largely [English](/wiki/English_Australians "English Australians"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Australians "Irish Australians"), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Australians "Scottish Australians") and [German](/wiki/German_Australians "German Australians"), while there was a wave of immigration from [southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and eastern Europe (most notably [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Australian "Italian Australian")) in the decades following the [second world war](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). In the 21st century, [Asia](/wiki/Asian_Australians "Asian Australians") (most notably [China](/wiki/Chinese_Australian "Chinese Australian") and [India](/wiki/Indian_Australian "Indian Australian")) has been the primary source of immigration.

At the 2016 census, the most commonly nominated ancestries were:[[N 3]](#cite_note-89)[[83]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-86)[[84]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-87)

* [English](/wiki/English_Australians "English Australians") (41.3%)
* Australian (37.9%)[[N 4]](#cite_note-91)
* [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Australians "Irish Australians") (13%)
* [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Australians "Scottish Australians") (11.2%)
* [German](/wiki/German_Australians "German Australians") (6.8%)
* [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") (4%)[[N 5]](#cite_note-92)
* [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Australians "Chinese Australians") (3.1%)
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Australians "Italian Australians") (3%)
* [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Australians "Indian Australians") (1.7%)
* [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Australians "Dutch Australians") (1.6%)
* [New Zealander](/wiki/New_Zealand_Australians "New Zealand Australians") (1.6%)
* [Maori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Australians "Māori Australians") (1.2%)
* [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_Australians "Filipino Australians") (1.2%)

The 2016 census showed that 28.9% of Queensland's inhabitants were [born overseas](/wiki/Immigration_to_Australia "Immigration to Australia"). Only 54.8% of inhabitants had both parents born in Australia, with the next most common birthplaces being New Zealand, England, [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") and South Africa.[[83]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-86)[[84]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-87) Brisbane has the [26th largest immigrant population](/wiki/Foreign_born#Metropolitan_and_Urban_regions_with_largest_foreign-born_populations "Foreign born") among world metropolitan areas.

4% of the population, or 186,482 people, identified as [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") ([Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders")) in 2016.[[N 6]](#cite_note-93)[[83]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-86)[[84]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-87)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Language")]

At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), 81.2% of inhabitants spoke only English at home, with the next most common languages being [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (1.5%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (0.6%), [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (0.5%), Spanish (0.4%) and Italian (0.4%).[[86]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au2-94)[[87]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au2-95)

At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), 80.5% of inhabitants spoke only English at home, with the next most common languages being [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (1.6%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (0.6%), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (0.6%) and Spanish (0.6%).[[88]](#cite_note-census2021-96)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Religion")]

At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), the most commonly cited religious affiliations were ['No religion'](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (29.2%), [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Australia "Catholic Church in Australia") (21.7%) and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia") (15.3%).[[89]](#cite_note-97) In the 2016 Census the majority of Queenslanders were identified as Christian, most of which were of various Protestant denominations.[[90]](#cite_note-m906-98)

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), 45.7% of the population follows Christianity, and 41.2% identified as having [No religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion")[[88]](#cite_note-census2021-96)[[91]](#cite_note-99) About 5% of people are affiliated with a non-Christian religion, mainly [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (1.4%), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") (1.3%) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1.2%).[[88]](#cite_note-census2021-96) The 2021 census found that Protestants of various denominations outnumbered Catholics in Queensland.[[92]](#cite_note-b868-100)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/University_of_Queensland.jpg/250px-University_of_Queensland.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Queensland.jpg)

The Great Court at the [University of Queensland](/wiki/University_of_Queensland "University of Queensland") in Brisbane, Queensland's oldest university

Queensland is home to numerous universities. The state's oldest university, the [University of Queensland](/wiki/University_of_Queensland "University of Queensland"), was established in 1909 and frequently [ranks among the world's top 50](/wiki/College_and_university_rankings "College and university rankings").[[93]](#cite_note-101)[[94]](#cite_note-102)[[95]](#cite_note-103) Other major universities include [Queensland University of Technology](/wiki/Queensland_University_of_Technology "Queensland University of Technology"), [Griffith University](/wiki/Griffith_University "Griffith University"), the [University of Southern Queensland](/wiki/University_of_Southern_Queensland "University of Southern Queensland"), the [University of the Sunshine Coast](/wiki/University_of_the_Sunshine_Coast "University of the Sunshine Coast"), [James Cook University](/wiki/James_Cook_University "James Cook University") (which was the state's first university outside of [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland")), [Central Queensland University](/wiki/Central_Queensland_University "Central Queensland University") and [Bond University](/wiki/Bond_University "Bond University") (which was Australia's first private university).

[International education](/wiki/International_student "International student") is an important industry, with 134,312 [international students enrolled in the state](/wiki/International_students_in_Australia "International students in Australia") in 2018, largely focused on Brisbane. Most of the state's international students are from Asia.[[96]](#cite_note-IntlStudying-104)

At the primary and secondary levels, Queensland is home to numerous [state](/wiki/Queensland_state_schools "Queensland state schools") and private schools.

Queensland has a [public library system](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_Queensland#Public_libraries "List of libraries in Queensland") which is managed by the State Library of Queensland.[[97]](#cite_note-105) Some university libraries are also open to the public.

## Economy

In 2019, Queensland had a [gross state product](/wiki/List_of_Australian_states_and_territories_by_gross_state_product "List of Australian states and territories by gross state product") of A$357,044 million, the [third-highest in the nation](/wiki/List_of_Australian_states_and_territories_by_gross_state_product "List of Australian states and territories by gross state product") after New South Wales and [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)").[[98]](#cite_note-106) The construction of [sea ports](/wiki/Sea_port "Sea port") and railways along Queensland's coast in the 19th century set up the foundations for the state's export-oriented mining and agricultural sectors. Since the 1980s, a sizeable influx of interstate and overseas migrants, large amounts of federal government investment, increased mining of vast mineral deposits and an expanding aerospace sector have contributed to the state's economic growth.[[99]](#cite_note-aus-107)

[Primary industries](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_industry "Primary sector of industry") include bananas, [pineapples](/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple"), peanuts, a wide variety of other tropical and temperate fruit and vegetables, grain crops, [wineries](/wiki/Winery "Winery"), cattle raising, cotton, [sugarcane](/wiki/Sugarcane "Sugarcane"), and [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool"). The mining industry includes [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite"), coal, silver, lead, [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), gold and copper.[[100]](#cite_note-108)[[101]](#cite_note-109)

[Secondary industries](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy") are mostly further processing of the above-mentioned primary produce. For example, bauxite is shipped by sea from [Weipa](/wiki/Weipa,_Queensland "Weipa, Queensland") and converted to alumina at [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone,_Queensland "Gladstone, Queensland").[[102]](#cite_note-rta-110) There is also copper refining and the refining of sugar cane to sugar at a number of mills along the eastern coastline.

Major [tertiary industries](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") are retail, tourism, and [international education](/wiki/International_student "International student"). In 2018, there were 134,312 [international students enrolled in the state](/wiki/International_students_in_Australia "International students in Australia"), largely focused on Brisbane. Most of the state's international students are from Asia.[[96]](#cite_note-IntlStudying-104)

Brisbane is [categorised](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") as a [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city"), and is among Asia-Pacific [cities with largest GDPs](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP"). It has strengths in mining, banking, insurance, transportation, information technology, real estate and [food](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry").[[103]](#cite_note-111) Some of the largest companies headquartered in Brisbane, all among Australia's largest, include [Suncorp Group](/wiki/Suncorp_Group "Suncorp Group"), [Virgin Australia](/wiki/Virgin_Australia "Virgin Australia"), [Aurizon](/wiki/Aurizon "Aurizon"), [Bank of Queensland](/wiki/Bank_of_Queensland "Bank of Queensland"), [Flight Centre](/wiki/Flight_Centre "Flight Centre"), [CUA](/wiki/CUA_(company) "CUA (company)"), [Sunsuper](/wiki/Sunsuper "Sunsuper"), [QSuper](/wiki/QSuper "QSuper"), [Domino's Pizza Enterprises](/wiki/Domino%27s_Pizza_Enterprises "Domino's Pizza Enterprises"), [Star Entertainment Group](/wiki/Star_Entertainment_Group "Star Entertainment Group"), [ALS](/wiki/ALS_Limited "ALS Limited"), [TechnologyOne](/wiki/TechnologyOne "TechnologyOne"), [NEXTDC](/wiki/NEXTDC "NEXTDC"), [Super Retail Group](/wiki/Super_Retail_Group "Super Retail Group"), [New Hope Coal](/wiki/New_Hope_Coal "New Hope Coal"), [Jumbo Interactive](/wiki/Jumbo_Interactive "Jumbo Interactive"), [National Storage](/wiki/National_Storage "National Storage"), [Collins Foods](/wiki/Collins_Foods "Collins Foods") and [Boeing Australia](/wiki/Boeing_Australia "Boeing Australia").[[104]](#cite_note-112)

### Tourism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Whitsunday_Islands_National_Park_%2823720070439%29.jpg/250px-Whitsunday_Islands_National_Park_%2823720070439%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whitsunday_Islands_National_Park_(23720070439).jpg)

Hill Inlet at the [Whitsunday Islands](/wiki/Whitsunday_Islands "Whitsunday Islands").

As a result of its varied landscapes, warm climate, and abundant natural environment, tourism is Queensland's leading tertiary industry with millions of interstate and international visitors visiting the state each year. The industry generates $8.8 billion annually, accounting for 4.5% of Queensland's Gross State Product. It has an annual export of $4.0 billion annually. The sector directly employs about 5.7% of Queensland citizens.[[105]](#cite_note-qtprofile-113) Accommodation in Queensland caters for nearly 22% of the total expenditure, followed by restaurants/meals (15%), airfares (11%), fuel (11%) and shopping/gifts (11%).[[106]](#cite_note-114)

The most visited tourist destinations of Queensland include Brisbane (including [Moreton](/wiki/Moreton_Island "Moreton Island") and [South Stradbroke](/wiki/South_Stradbroke_Island "South Stradbroke Island") islands and the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland")) as well as the [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland"), the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns"), [Port Douglas](/wiki/Port_Douglas,_Queensland "Port Douglas, Queensland"), the [Daintree Rainforest](/wiki/Daintree_Rainforest "Daintree Rainforest"), [K'gari](/wiki/K%27gari_(Fraser_Island) "K'gari (Fraser Island)") and the [Whitsunday Islands](/wiki/Whitsunday_Islands "Whitsunday Islands").[[107]](#cite_note-LonelyPlanet-115)[[108]](#cite_note-AirportRentals-116)[[109]](#cite_note-TravelTreks-117)

The [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") is home to numerous popular surf beaches such as those at [Surfers Paradise](/wiki/Surfers_Paradise,_Queensland "Surfers Paradise, Queensland") and [Burleigh Heads](/wiki/Burleigh_Heads,_Queensland "Burleigh Heads, Queensland"). It also includes the largest concentration of [amusement parks](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park") in Australia, including [Dreamworld](/wiki/Dreamworld_(Australian_theme_park) "Dreamworld (Australian theme park)"), [Movie World](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Movie_World "Warner Bros. The [Sunshine Coast](/wiki/Sunshine_Coast,_Queensland "Sunshine Coast, Queensland") includes popular surfing and beach destinations including [Noosa Heads](/wiki/Noosa_Heads,_Queensland "Noosa Heads, Queensland") and [Mooloolaba](/wiki/Mooloolaba "Mooloolaba"). It is also home to [UnderWater World](/wiki/UnderWater_World,_Queensland "UnderWater World, Queensland") and [Steve Irwin](/wiki/Steve_Irwin "Steve Irwin")'s [Australia Zoo](/wiki/Australia_Zoo "Australia Zoo"). Its hinterland includes the [Glass House Mountains National Park](/wiki/Glass_House_Mountains_National_Park "Glass House Mountains National Park").[[114]](#cite_note-122)

[Cairns](/wiki/Cairns "Cairns") is renowned as the gateway to the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), [Far North Queensland](/wiki/Far_North_Queensland "Far North Queensland") (including [Port Douglas](/wiki/Port_Douglas,_Queensland "Port Douglas, Queensland")) and the [Daintree Rainforest](/wiki/Daintree_Rainforest "Daintree Rainforest"). The [Whitsunday Islands](/wiki/Whitsunday_Islands "Whitsunday Islands") off the coast of [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland") are a popular tourist destinations for their [resort](/wiki/Resort "Resort") facilities and access to the Great Barrier Reef.[[115]](#cite_note-123)

## Politics and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Politics and government")]

Main article: [Politics of Queensland](/wiki/Politics_of_Queensland "Politics of Queensland")

One of the six founding [states of Australia](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"), Queensland has been a [federated state](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state") subject to the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") since 1 January 1901. It may legislate on all matters not [ceded in the Australian Constitution to the federal government](/wiki/Section_51_of_the_Constitution_of_Australia "Section 51 of the Constitution of Australia"). It is a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). The [Constitution of Queensland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Queensland "Constitution of Queensland") sets out the operation of the state's government. The state's constitution contains several [entrenched provisions](/wiki/Entrenched_clause "Entrenched clause") which cannot be changed in the absence of a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum"). There is also a [statutory](/wiki/Statutory_law "Statutory law") [charter of rights](/wiki/Charter_of_rights "Charter of rights"), the *Queensland Human Rights Act 2019*. Queensland's system of government is influenced by the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") and [Australia's federal system of government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia").

The government power [can be divided](/wiki/Separation_of_powers_in_Australia "Separation of powers in Australia") into three groups:

* Legislature: the [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") [Parliament of Queensland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Queensland "Parliament of Queensland"), comprising the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Queensland "Legislative Assembly of Queensland") and the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") (represented by the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland"));
* Executive: the [Queensland Government](/wiki/Queensland_Government "Queensland Government"), which consists of the [Executive Council of Queensland](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Queensland "Executive Council of Queensland"), which formalises decisions of the [Cabinet of Queensland](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Queensland "Cabinet of Queensland"), which is composed of the [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland") and other ministers of state appointed by the Governor on the advice of the premier;
* Judiciary: the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Queensland "Supreme Court of Queensland") and other state courts, whose judges are appointed by the Governor on the advice of Parliament.

[Executive authority](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") is nominally vested in the [Governor of Queensland](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland") (currently [Jeannette Young](/wiki/Jeannette_Young "Jeannette Young")) who represents and is appointed by the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") (currently [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")) on the advice of the [Premier of Queensland](/wiki/Premier_of_Queensland "Premier of Queensland"). The Premier, who is the state's [Head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), along with the [Cabinet of Queensland](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Queensland "Cabinet of Queensland") (whose decisions are formalised by the [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_Queensland "Executive Council of Queensland")), exercise executive authority in practice. The Premier is appointed by the Governor and [must have support](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") of the [Legislative Assembly of Queensland](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Queensland "Legislative Assembly of Queensland"). The Premier is in practice a leading member of the Legislative Assembly and parliamentary leader of his or her political party, or coalition of parties, and members of the Cabinet will be drawn from the same party or coalition. The current Premier and [Deputy Premier](/wiki/Deputy_Premier_of_Queensland "Deputy Premier of Queensland") are [David Crisafulli](/wiki/David_Crisafulli "David Crisafulli") and [Jarrod Bleijie](/wiki/Jarrod_Bleijie "Jarrod Bleijie") of the [Liberal National Party](/wiki/Liberal_National_Party_of_Queensland "Liberal National Party of Queensland") respectively. [Government House](/wiki/Government_House,_Brisbane "Government House, Brisbane") at [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_Queensland "Paddington, Queensland") in Brisbane is the seat of the Governor, having replaced [Old Government House](/wiki/Old_Government_House,_Queensland "Old Government House, Queensland") at [Gardens Point](/wiki/Gardens_Point,_Brisbane "Gardens Point, Brisbane") in Brisbane's [CBD](/wiki/Brisbane_central_business_district "Brisbane central business district") in the early 20th century. The executive branch is simply referred to as the [Queensland Government](/wiki/Queensland_Government "Queensland Government").

[Legislative authority](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") is exercised by the [Queensland Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Queensland "Parliament of Queensland") which uniquely for Australian states is [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism"), containing only one house, the Legislative Assembly. The Parliament was [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral_legislature "Bicameral legislature") until 1922 when the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Queensland_Legislative_Council "Queensland Legislative Council") was abolished by the Labor "suicide squad", so called because they were appointed for the purpose of voting to abolish their own offices.[[116]](#cite_note-apg-124) [Bills](/wiki/Bill_(law) "Bill (law)") receive [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") from the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Queensland "Governor of Queensland") before being passed into law. The Parliament's seat is at [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Brisbane "Parliament House, Brisbane") at [Gardens Point](/wiki/Gardens_Point,_Brisbane "Gardens Point, Brisbane") in Brisbane's CBD. Members of the Legislative Assembly represent [93 electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_districts_of_Queensland "Electoral districts of Queensland"). Elections in Queensland are held at the end of each fixed four-year parliamentary term and are determined by full [preferential voting](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting").

The state's [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") consists of the [Supreme Court of Queensland](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Queensland "Supreme Court of Queensland") and the [District Court of Queensland](/wiki/District_Court_of_Queensland "District Court of Queensland"), established by the Queensland Constitution, as well as the [Magistrates Court of Queensland](/wiki/Magistrates_Court_of_Queensland "Magistrates Court of Queensland") and other courts and tribunals established by legislation. Cases may be appealed to the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia"). As with all Australian states and territories, Queensland has a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") legal system. The Supreme and District courts are headquartered at the [Queen Elizabeth II Courts of Law](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Courts_of_Law,_Brisbane "Queen Elizabeth II Courts of Law, Brisbane") in Brisbane's CBD.

The state's politics are traditionally regarded as being [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Australia "Conservatism in Australia") relative to other states.[[117]](#cite_note-Daly,_Leffman,_Dehne_2003_p397-125)[[118]](#cite_note-Penrith,_Seal_2008_p478-126)[[119]](#cite_note-127)[[120]](#cite_note-128)[[121]](#cite_note-129) Historically, the lack of an [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the "[Bjelkemander](/wiki/Bjelkemander "Bjelkemander")" (a [malapportion](/wiki/Malapportionment "Malapportionment") favouring rural electoral districts) has meant that Queensland had a long tradition of domination by strong-willed, [populist](/wiki/Populism "Populism") premiers, often accused of authoritarian tendencies, holding office for long periods. This tendency was exemplified by the government of the state's longest-serving Premier [Joh Bjelke-Petersen](/wiki/Joh_Bjelke-Petersen "Joh Bjelke-Petersen").

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Queensland&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Local government")]

Main article: [Local government in Queensland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Queensland "Local government in Queensland")

Local government is the mechanism by which [local government areas](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_Queensland "Local government areas of Queensland") can manage their own affairs to the extent permitted by the Local Government Act 2009. Queensland is divided into 77 local government areas, which are created by the state government under the legislation.[[122]](#cite_note-130) Each local government area has a council responsible for providing a range of local services and utilities. Local councils derive their income from both rates and charges on resident ratepayers, and grants and subsidies from the state and federal governments.[[123]](#cite_note-131)
